speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is October 25, 1995. We are interviewing the survivor Mr. Milton S. Hirschfeld. Interviewer, Anita White. We are in the city of Oceanside, New York, in USA. The language of the interview is English. My name is Anita White. I'm interviewing the survivor Mr. Milton S. Hirschfeld. The interview is on October 25, 1995. We are in Oceanside, New York. Would you please introduce yourself, give us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Milton S. Hirschfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you kindly spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-I-R-S-C-H-F-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any other names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- only they called me-- my name was given at the birth, Samuel. And I was called [? Milek. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other nickname later during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, I used for some time the name [? Sigmund. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The birthday is February the 27th, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radlow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town near Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell the name of your city, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's R-A-D-L-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Hirschfeld, what was the name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my father was Markus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozalia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters, the older was Sala. And the younger was Polda.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in the building industry at the early stages. Later on, he went to Belgium for a few years to learn the diamond trade. And when he came back, he was also a broker in the diamond business, and an administrator of houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you comfortable at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started the first grade, it was in Mizrahi, at the-- at the age of six. Then I went to public schools for three years. And then after the three years, I went to the gymnasium, which is a middle school, for eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you graduate that middle school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you received matura?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I applied to University of Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you accepted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, originally I applied for the-- to the medical school. But of course I was not accepted, because of the numerus--

speaker: interviewer
text: --clausus.

speaker: interviewee
text: --clausus, which was at that time. And despite the fact that I was the valedictorian, and I had all the best grades, I was not accepted. So I applied to the University to the philosophical department at the main subject, chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you explain numerus clausus to us please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school in Cracow, the medical school, accepted 120 people, 120 students every year. But the numerus clausus, it was a closed quota for Jewish people. The same as the percentage of the population, which means 10 year-- 10%, which would be 12. And it was 12 probably the first year. And then it diminished, diminished, diminished, as finally, at the year that I applied, only two were accepted. Which were children of doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at that university, at [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you interrupt your studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't interrupt, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. What type of home did you grow up in, was it very religious, or just observant, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an observant home, but not very religious. My father was originally very, very religious, but after he came back from abroad, he was already-- he gave up certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At my home, with my mother, I spoke Polish. And my father spoke Polish, too. But sometimes he spoke Yiddish, he wanted to say something. But normally he spoke Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to the sports club, Maccabi while I was in the school. I played basketball, and volleyball, or soccer sometimes. And during my years at the university, I belonged to a Zionist organization, Kadima.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please tell us a little bit about the Jewish life in Cracow before the war, about your friendships, and about if any influences of antisemitism on your personal life and what you know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a neighborhood, which was the edge of the Jewish section. When we lived on Josefa Street. Across from me was the Hebrew gymnasium, which was known it was a Zionist organization. And of course nobody of religious people belonged there, or was there. But we were on the edge of this-- of this-- of this so-called Cracow section, Jewish section.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the Jewish section, was there a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kazimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kazimierz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And actually, all the center section of the Jewish-- they called it ghetto at that time-- was the Krakowska Street, where the Jewish community administration was, where the Jewish hospital was located. But in all these sections, there were-- there lived a lot of Poles also.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you personally experience any antisemitism? Were you aware of any antisemitism at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know some other people in the classrooms did experience. But I didn't, because as I mentioned before, I was a-- the first student, excellent student, and everybody wanted to be on good relations with me. So I didn't-- I didn't feel it so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware, or do you recall, whether there was any awareness in Cracow in the Jewish community of the danger coming from, from the Nazi-- Nazis on the West? And did anybody try to run away or do something about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could think of this, what happened. So people couldn't do anything. There are-- there-- I know there were some people who saved themselves because they went to the United States for the World's Fair. But this was just their luck that they were away when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did they go away with whole families? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They didn't go. Later on, when the Germans were already in, within one year, for money you could get out through Czechoslovakia, to Hungary. You could go all around, and I know families were-- which had-- which did it. But not whole families. But usually they send out the men only, because this was the danger. So the men left, and the rest of the family stayed there. And I had very close-- I knew very close people, where the men-- all the men, four brothers, were saved in America. And from one family, all women also saved themselves because they were at Schindler. But from the other one, no one saved their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you recall the outbreak of the war? Tell us what happened when the war broke out. And what do you remember those times-- of those times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the first thing that I remember is that it broke out the 1st of September. And everybody was on vacation because the vacations ended on, on the 1st of-- 1st of September or a little bit later. So everybody was on vacation, and I was also on vacation in the mountains. But a few days before, when we felt that, that it-- broke-- the war would break out at any minute, we came back. It was hardly-- we could get a-- you could not get a place in the train. I remember the whole few hours, six hours, on the train, I was standing on the steps outside of the-- of the cars. And finally we arrived home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at first, we with my sister that we will try to run away toward the East. So there was a, a, a wagon. We had a wagon with four horses, and there were about five families who were there also. And we tried to go away. But the Germans were much quicker. And on the third day, we understood already from the people who told us that they are ahead of us already to the East, and that's why we turned 180 degrees. And we came back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody came back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All the people were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where-- what happens then? Now it's the beginning of the war, and you're in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got stuck in Cracow. So the first thing we, we tried to-- we never expected from the Germans. Nobody expected what they saw even the first days because of the brutality. They were all super brutal, in every respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught people on the street for work. You couldn't get out in the morning because they needed work. And the latrines, and the magazines, and the warehouses, to unload and to load the stuff and this. The first day when they came in near the fortress, Wawel, they took all the people from the stores around. All the men from all stores around, even not Jewish people, and they sent them to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. And this was the first thing that we knew that there's a concentration camp, Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they use the term already, concentration camp, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they know to Buchenwald. We knew that Buchenwald is concentration camp. And they-- and especially we were touched because one of my uncles with his son were sent out at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you caught in that net of working, work? What happened? What do you do? What does your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, we, we tried-- we tried not to get out from the house. But finally, you did have-- you did go out of the house. So you-- if you are caught once, they caught you, so you had to work the whole day. And you came back in the evening you got at least your meal. But otherwise, the, the brutality was extreme. Sometimes, for instance, soldiers, they took on the street, they caught especially in the Jewish neighborhood, they caught all the people which were long beards. And they had a machine and scissors, and they cut. But they didn't cut the whole beard. They cut only half of it, and they let them go like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was looking for some type of work. And I got work in the Jewish community. They created something new which was a resettlement office. We understood that the Germans wanted to resettle some people from the-- to the East, to East Poland where there was more space. And they wanted to have certain areas here for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they at that resettlement office which they created-- was it attached to the old kehilla, or did they create something new?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they created right, right away something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish community. There was a president and a vice-president and a board.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they it-- they called it in Polish [POLISH]. But that's what they-- that's what they wanted. The reason that they created this is they're supposed to be the liaison between the population and the Germans. Whatever they wanted to order, they ordered through them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of-- in German of that [POLISH], as you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they start calling it anything else but? Do you know the term Judenrat? Did they ever call it Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they called it Judenrat also. They-- I didn't pay attention to it. It was the same thing. They were same people, more or less, and in the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the job in the Juden-- in the resettlement office? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the resettlement office, we supposed to start giving out identification, iden-- identification card for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: For everybody, men, women, and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: For everybody who had to work. And according to the Germans, everybody had to work. Because they called the women also. For washing floors and even for carrying. For-- the same thing as they did later in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of cards were you giving out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We gave cards with pictures and we put the name in, and where anybody lives. And later on, when they-- when they issued some permits to go out of the ghetto, for instance, you had to have this card, and, and it was attached to this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Judenrat immediately start to provide some food distribution to the people there, to the Jews there? Or how did people live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't-- the distribution of the food was normally from the beginning. I mean, there was a baker, and the baker--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to buy it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Apparently from the Judenrat he received a-- he received farina or the-- I mean, the, the, the things that he needed for baking bread, and for baking anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if people wanted to buy some food, they had to go to the places to buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: To buy food. You could buy. The stores were open.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did the poor people do then, in those areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the poor people, if they didn't have money, they tried to sell whatever they had. But if they didn't have money, they went to the Judenrat just to tell them that they don't have money and they need something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Judenrat establish any soup kitchens, or any help for those people in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the soup kitchen was always in the place there where the Judenrat was. There was always a soup kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was your father doing at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father did whatever he did before. He was an administrator.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he administering now, as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Houses. Houses, yes. Because there were still Jews who, who had houses. And they collected rents from the houses. As of this time, they still collected. As long as they were in Cracow, they collected. As a matter of fact, he had two, two, two accounts where they were older women, Jewish women, who were-- who were home-bound. They couldn't move. They couldn't do anything, and they depended on this. You could have gone to the bank, and to put money in the bank, and to take out money in the bank. According to what was allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall, even that early, were there any [GERMAN] in Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: So early, there were no [GERMAN], but there were sometimes, a section was closed. And they came in to all the houses and told the people that they have to give all the furs. They couldn't be allowed to have furs. Then they came in once, and they-- we have to give back some money, but this was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us about the early days. When did they create a Jewish police, if there was an ordnungsdienst? Was there a Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- from the beginning, there was no ordnungsdienst. Yet. But a little bit later, they created it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So please--

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Judenrat received order, whatever order the Germans wanted to give, except the first one, that they-- that they told that everybody had to have arm bands with the Jewish sign on them. Except this one, which the governor gave out. Anything what they wanted, if they wanted some money to be collected, if they wanted-- whatever. They went to the Judenrat, and the Judenrat had to do it. There was like a-- there was a German SS officer who was especially assigned for this to the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- were there any medical services, any hospitals, anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a Jewish hospital near the Judenrat place, where it was before the war. It was a Jewish hospital which had all facilities, and everything which was needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this more or less calm situation exist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well this situation existed until the ghetto was supposed to be opened. Until the ghetto was supposed to be started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the next year, in 1940, they already announced that there will be a ghetto. And the ghetto will be only people who have an interest to be in Cracow-- or there in the ghetto-- will be put in the ghetto. The rest wouldn't have-- wouldn't have the permission. So everybody was, was afraid what they will do with the other people. But they told them that they can go and to live in the little villages around Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what terrible tension, what tension did that situation created, because people didn't know what's going to be to live in Cracow, not to live in Cracow? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they had to-- they looked for work, to work somewhere. Because if you work, you had a chance to go to the ghetto. If you didn't work, you wouldn't go to the ghetto. Because they wouldn't allow you. Except is there were some people who, who had big interests-- they were big manufacturers-- who were a big interest in Cracow. So they allowed for these people, if they paid some amount, some big sums to the-- to the Judenrat, that they will get permission to stay either in ghetto, or even outside of the ghetto if needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head, do you recall the name of the head of the ghetto at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The head of the-- of the-- not of the ghetto. Of the Judenrat--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the ghetto, Judenrat I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat-- the head of the Judenrat was Marek Beiberstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: So please explain to us this, this-- the situation was paying for staying in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- a commission was created, with three consisted, consisted of three members.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who created the commission?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German administration. One was a delegate of the German administration of the city. One was the Germ-- a delegate of the Polish population. And one was the delegate of the Jewish population, which was the president, the Judenrat president.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were they accomplishing? What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started taking applications with the sums of money which everybody had to pay. And everybody had to show why he wants to stay. And of course there were a lot of people who put the money in. But this whole deal became sour after a few, few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: One question, please. did everybody have to pay in the same amount, or everybody was levied a different sum?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they levied a different sum. They knew how much-- as much as somebody could afford.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during the first week, or two weeks, everyone-- everything went all right. Applications were reviewed, and certain permissions were given, certain denied. But after this time, the German representative approached the Jewish representative and he told him that from now on, unless he gets some money from the money which is collected, no one of these applications will be approved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the German representative?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the German was Reichert. I remember the name because his son went to school, to gymnasium, with me. But I didn't know that he was a volksdeutsche.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you, you-- president of Judenrat is threatened that unless he gets money, they-- nothing will happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: No one of the application will go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: A moment ago, you said some applications were approved. Did those people whom they approved, did they get already something special, a special paper, a stamp, something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they were supposed to get the permission. They were supposed-- I don't know whether they were given items. Nobody knew about it. This was-- nobody wanted to publicize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about it. Yes. So what's happening then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then, when-- then they decided that they would have to give him some money because he wanted it as a bribe. He wanted to participate in this, and he wanted to have the money. So the president, the Jewish Judenrat president, gave him a part. They knew, he knew exactly how much money there was. And there were a big  sum of money. So he just to give him-- he met him in the car, and in the car, he gave him each time some money. Unless this old-- all of a sudden, the Germans knew about it. But of course, not the-- not the German representation of the city, but other people, somehow they knew about it. And they arrested Mr. Reichert, and Mr. Beiberstein. And there was a trial. And they got-- Mr. Beiberstein got 18 months prison in Montelupich, which was the worst prison, in Montelupich. And Reichert received only six months. For active. Reichert was for passive corruption, or bribe. And Mr. Beiberstein for active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who initiated this-- the, the, the entire trial? The whole thing. Who started that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Once this was revealed, they started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who took over the Judenrat then, do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- I think the vice president was Mr-- it was a lawyer, Goldblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now do you know anything more about this trial that you would like to tell us about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the trial was-- people normally didn't know about the trial, but I happened to know because I was befriended with the son of Mr.-- of Marek Beiberstein. And after the war, he explained to me that he resented-- actually he didn't resent. But he wanted to correct a misrepresentation. Because it was written, Mr. Keneally in his book mentioned that it was for money manipulations. Which was a-- which was not the right thing which was happened to them. So he explained it to me from A to Z exactly what had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it Mr Beiberstein, Jr.'s, opinion that his father did it because he wanted to try to save some Jews? That was the idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Of course. He was, as far as his character was, he was 100% He was not a rich man. He didn't request any money, he didn't take any money, any penny from this, because everything was in the books. He didn't take anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the money went directly to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the money to the Germans except--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or to Mr. Reichert,

speaker: interviewee
text: --or the rest which was remaining, which people needed for moving people around. For keeping hospitals, for keeping everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Mr. Beiberstein after the jail? Did he come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the jail, he came out. After 18 months, he came out. So it was in '41 already. And then they initiated another trial that it was-- that he dealt-- the [GERMAN] started this. That he was not supposed to have money, the Jews are not supposed to have money. And they started this. But there was a professor of the university who was-- who defended him in this trial, and he was acquitted. But he came back to the ghetto, and he lived in the ghetto a couple of two or three months, and he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- I would like to take you back now to the ghetto again, or the beginnings of the ghetto. People stay in Cracow, and people go outside to the suburbs. Please, what happens to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went outside. Because my father couldn't get any permission to go to ghetto, because he was already 60 years old, or almost 60. So we couldn't go. So we took-- we were in the suburbs of Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: A special place?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a sp-- a village, Borek Falecki. That's how they called it. And we lived there, from-- we had to move there right away. In other words, in '40. And we stayed there. Actually, we moved there in '41. In the beginning of '41. And we stayed there until we had to move. Until after the ghetto was created. And we had to move into the ghetto. Because they didn't allow to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was that, when they created the ghetto, more or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: They created the ghetto at the end of 1941.
